questions.question: In a distributed trace flame graph, you see the checkout API taking 4 seconds total with one child span (Inventory Service PostgreSQL query) taking 3.6 seconds. What is the correct action?
options: Scale up the API Gateway to reduce the 4-second response time.

Investigate the specific PostgreSQL query in the Inventory Service — it is the root cause consuming 90% of the total request time. Without distributed tracing, you'd only know 'the checkout API is slow' and would have to guess which of the 12 services to investigate.
Add a Redis cache in front of the API Gateway to reduce overall latency.
Increase the timeout threshold for the checkout API from 4 seconds to 8 seconds.
questions.answer: Investigate the specific PostgreSQL query in the Inventory Service — it is the root cause consuming 90% of the total request time. Without distributed tracing, you'd only know 'the checkout API is slow' and would have to guess which of the 12 services to investigate.
questions.explanation: This is exactly what distributed tracing provides: instant root cause identification from a flame graph rather than hours of log grepping across 12 different service log streams.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'traceparent' HTTP header in the W3C Trace Context standard?
options: It carries authentication tokens to allow downstream services to make API calls.

It propagates the Trace ID and parent Span ID to downstream services, so every service in the request's path creates child spans under the same trace — building a complete end-to-end call tree rather than disconnected per-service traces.
It sets the HTTP priority level to ensure traced requests are processed faster.
It enables HTTP/2 multiplexing for services that participate in a distributed trace.
questions.answer: It propagates the Trace ID and parent Span ID to downstream services, so every service in the request's path creates child spans under the same trace — building a complete end-to-end call tree rather than disconnected per-service traces.
questions.explanation: Without traceparent propagation, each service would generate its own independent trace. You'd see 12 separate Jaeger traces with no connection between them — useless for debugging cross-service latency.

questions.question: Why is Grafana Tempo more cost-efficient at large scale than traditional trace backends like Elasticsearch-based solutions?
options: Tempo compresses traces using a proprietary algorithm that Elasticsearch cannot replicate.

Tempo stores traces in cheap object storage (S3/GCS) rather than building expensive search indexes. Traces are retrieved by Trace ID (which costs nothing to index). This makes Tempo dramatically cheaper than full-text indexed solutions at scale, since terabytes of trace data in S3 cost pennies while Elasticsearch indexing is expensive.
Tempo runs as a serverless function, eliminating infrastructure costs entirely.
Tempo only stores the last 24 hours of traces, reducing storage requirements.
questions.answer: Tempo stores traces in cheap object storage (S3/GCS) rather than building expensive search indexes. Traces are retrieved by Trace ID (which costs nothing to index). This makes Tempo dramatically cheaper than full-text indexed solutions at scale, since terabytes of trace data in S3 cost pennies while Elasticsearch indexing is expensive.
questions.explanation: At 100M traces/day, Elasticsearch-based tracing can cost tens of thousands of dollars per month in storage and indexing. Tempo with S3 backend can handle the same volume for hundreds of dollars.
